name: RunPod API
what_it_does: RunPod is a GPU cloud platform with a GraphQL API and Python SDK for deploying serverless GPU inference endpoints, spinning up persistent GPU pod instances, and running batch AI/ML workloads — enabling agents to provision compute and invoke ML models on demand.
use_cases: Invoking RunPod Serverless endpoints to run GPU-accelerated ML inference (image generation, LLM serving, embeddings) as agent tool calls with auto-scaling
Provisioning and managing persistent GPU pod instances for long-running ML training jobs via the GraphQL API
Submitting batch inference jobs to a RunPod Serverless endpoint and polling for results to process large queues of inputs in parallel
Deploying custom Docker containers with ML models as RunPod Serverless workers and routing agent inference requests through the endpoint
Querying GPU availability and pricing to programmatically select cost-optimal instance types for training workload scheduling
not_for: CPU-only workloads — RunPod is GPU-focused; CPU-only tasks are better served by cheaper commodity compute providers
Always-on low-latency APIs requiring sub-200ms cold starts — RunPod serverless has cold start latency when scaling from zero workers
Enterprise-grade compliance environments — RunPod has limited compliance certifications compared to hyperscalers; not suitable for HIPAA/PCI workloads without additional controls
best_when: An agent needs on-demand GPU compute for AI/ML inference or training without managing Kubernetes or cloud infrastructure, and cost is a priority over enterprise SLAs.
avoid_when: You need enterprise compliance certifications, sub-200ms p99 latency guarantees, or purely CPU-based compute.

auth.notes: Authentication uses a personal API key passed in the Authorization header as Bearer token for REST endpoints, or as a query parameter for the GraphQL API. The Python SDK accepts the API key via runpod.api_key = 'key' or RUNPOD_API_KEY environment variable. Keys are account-scoped with no fine-grained permissions or scoping.

pricing.free_tier_limits: No free tier. New account credit promotions may apply periodically.
pricing.paid_tiers: Serverless GPU pricing from ~$0.00019/second for RTX 3090 to ~$0.00080/second for A100-80GB. Spot instances available at 50-80% discount with interruption risk. Pod pricing varies by GPU type and whether on-demand or spot.
pricing.notes: Credit card required at signup. Billing is usage-based with per-second granularity. Spot instances offer significant cost savings but can be interrupted — agent workflows using spot must implement checkpoint/resume logic. Community cloud (spot) vs Secure Cloud (on-demand) distinction is important for reliability.

performance.rate_limits: Serverless endpoint concurrency limited by worker count and max_workers configuration. Cold start latency (scaling from 0 workers) ranges from 10-60 seconds depending on container size and GPU availability. API management calls have no published rate limits.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL API returns standard GraphQL error objects with message arrays. REST API errors are less consistent — some return structured JSON, others plain text. Serverless job status includes error fields when jobs fail but the error messages from the worker container are passed through raw without normalization.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Serverless job submissions create new jobs each time — there is no idempotency key mechanism. Pod provisioning operations are also non-idempotent. Agents must track job IDs externally and check status before resubmitting to avoid duplicate work.
known_agent_gotchas: Cold start latency for serverless workers can be 10-60 seconds when scaling from zero — agents must implement polling with appropriate timeouts (minimum 60-120 seconds) rather than expecting immediate responses from GPU inference endpoints.

Community Cloud (spot) GPU pods can be interrupted with minimal notice — agents using spot instances for training must implement checkpointing within their workloads; RunPod does not automatically handle interruption recovery.

Serverless endpoint URLs include an endpoint ID that changes if the endpoint is deleted and recreated — agents storing endpoint URLs must handle the case where an endpoint is redeployed and its ID changes.

The GraphQL API is the primary interface for pod management operations — the Python SDK wraps this, but raw GraphQL mutations require knowing the specific mutation names and argument structures that are not always well-documented.

GPU availability for specific instance types can be intermittent, especially for high-demand GPUs like A100s — agents that hard-code GPU type requirements may fail with 'no workers available' errors and need fallback GPU type logic.

security_score_components.security_notes: API keys are account-scoped with no permission granularity — a leaked key allows full account access including spending funds and terminating workloads. No built-in secret injection mechanism for worker containers (secrets must be passed via environment variables in pod/endpoint configuration). TLS enforced on all endpoints. No published compliance certifications.
